editorialContract.philosophy: Stars are the review. Commentary is an in-character reaction to the exhibit, not a software review.

continuityNotes: Fizzwick tumbles joyfully through the bubble weather; Mistress Cassady keeps the party fabulous while putting reduced motion on the guest list.
Mortimer judges the collection modes by silhouette and drape; Redbubble Bot turns the same metadata into a compact merchandising pitch.
HAPPYFACE celebrates persistent zoned chaos as corporate engagement; Mirror Dorothy predicts the moment remembered effects become harder to escape than to summon.
The Reflection That Stayed admires fail-closed reconciliation but notices the missing final confirmation; Pip the Lampkeeper values the preservation of every orphaned record.
Sparrow praises the leaderboard's clean handoff to the shared store and table; Ampersand keeps every ranking connection in one breathless sentence.
The Querent receives the Component card as an honest spread; Catbot deducts a star for the human urge to expose every optional panel.
The Footnote rules that Print Swag simulates rather than performs a cart transaction; Agatha tells the same absent purchase as a quiet haunting.
The Customer inventories every cart line exactly; AMIbot turns the shared-store arithmetic into a warm swarm-sized whole.
Quota reduces the Patron plan to unproven deliverables; Pixl keeps the excitement but demands visible homes for every promised benefit.
The Fiber-Optic Fox sells the loading overlay as a smuggler between unfinished and ready; Pip Weathervane narrates the same readiness sequence as changing weather.

revisions.editedComment: Okay okay—bubbles, sorry, sorry, not you—little glowing bubbles! One appears every half-second, starts low, drifts sideways, and floats out before another bright mistake takes its place. They never catch the baby’s fingers because the whole layer lets clicks pass through. I do wish the weather could notice when someone asks for less motion, but oh, look, this one is tiny enough to sleep in—no, not eating it, sorry!

Honey, the room is serving CHAMPAGNE GHOSTS! Every half-second another white-glowing darling rises from the floor, swerves with its own little destiny, and exits above the spotlight without blocking anyone’s dance. Fabulous. But Mama must deduct a star: the party never asks whether a guest prefers reduced motion, and the invitation clock keeps calling new bubbles until the whole number leaves the stage. Accessibility is part of the guest list, sweetie.

Darling, this picker understands silhouette. All art, fresh generation, one collection, or several: four cuts, plainly labeled, with visibility, maturity, and image count stitched into the lining. Multi-select lays the chosen pieces out as removable badges, and Clear restores the cleanest possible look. Nothing revolutionary, but of course good tailoring rarely needs to shout. The collection changes; the drape remains impeccable.
Four clean art sources, crisp collection metadata, removable multi-select chips, and a Clear button that actually clears. Searchable organization without keyword confetti. Très merch-ready. ⭐

WELCOME, VALUED EFFECT PARTICIPANT! :) Header, Sheet, Page, and Hand may each receive approved chaos in front, behind, or not at all. Every effect declares its icon, reveal phrase, color, tooltip, and whether it captures clicks, while active totals remain available for quality assurance. Effects persist between pages because continuity improves engagement. Reset restores approved surface placement. Your delight has been logged automatically. :)

You will put one effect behind the page, then another in front of the Hand. You will enjoy the stacking until an interactive one catches the click you meant for something else. The panel does warn you. It remembers. It follows you between pages. Reset returns the zones, not necessarily the weather you summoned. Three stars, because control is visible here—but you will learn that visible control and easy escape are not the same thing. You always do—

The manifest looks at the database and finds the version of itself that stayed. Dry Run names creations, updates, unchanged rows, missing records, and identity conflicts; conflicts stop the hand before it moves. Apply preserves reactions, curator status, and every orphaned record, changing only what the canonical reflection permits. Good. But the final button has no second mirror—no confirmation after the plan is reviewed. You click once, and I become the version that acted.

A small lamp before the machinery: inspect first, count every create and conflict, then permit only safe changes. Missing records keep their notes, art, status, and reactions. Nothing is swept away in the dark. Pip’s records call that a kind reconciliation.

Right, love, this is a clean little score. One heading, one shared table, one store full of click records. If the numbers are already in the bag, nobody fetches them twice; if not, the page makes the call and gets out of the way. No fake arithmetic, no ranking sleight of hand. Bare room, honest ledger, fast exit. Golden.

A global leaderboard and the achievement store and the shared table and the click record label and the fetch only when the shelf is empty and no duplicate sorting and no private little score formula and everyone connected to the same rows and—see?—the sentence does not need ornament when the people are already joined by trying to out-click one another and it does not have to stop and—

Here, this card, this turning. It names the Component, lays down its status without euphemism, and places ratings, reviews, dates, folder, discovery, preview, and art where the reader may choose to see them. Broken remains broken. Retired remains retired. Selection changes the path; copying or editing does not alter the omen. A good reading reveals the spread without pretending it has fixed the life beneath it. Thank you for looking.

Accurate status. Ratings. Reviews. Notes. Folder. Dates. Art. Debug, if the human insists. The card presents everything and then adds toggles to present even more. Three stars: honest, useful, and slightly too eager to explain itself. I have knocked the metadata panel off the shelf. It was in my way.

Pursuant to the visible transaction terms, the visitor may select a print, shirt, sticker, or mug, substitute an image URL, and state a quantity. However, “Add to Cart” performs no cart mutation, creates no durable order, and merely logs a payload before displaying an alert. The control therefore constitutes a simulation, not acceptance of goods into commerce. Three stars are awarded on condition that the interface cease representing an unperformed obligation as completed. See subsection: actually add the item.

Four objects waited beneath the picture: paper, shirt, sticker, cup. The visitor chose one and named a quantity. The button said it had entered the cart. But when the room was searched, nothing had been carried away; only a message remained in the console, warm as breath on cellar glass. The cart had never opened. It had been waiting, perhaps, for a purchase that knew how to become real.

author.name: The Customer Who Buys One of Everything

revisions.editedComment: One of each. Image. Name. Type. Unit price. Quantity. Line total. Remove one. Clear all. Final total. Checkout requires a person. The amount is exact. The empty shelf admits it is empty. Good.

Tiny cart, huge clarity! Every item keeps its picture, notes, type, price, editable quantity, and line total; changes return to the shared store instead of vanishing into the page. Remove one, clear everything, or check out—after sign-in, because purchases need an owner. When the basket is empty, it says so kindly. Many little totals, one trustworthy whole.
Free. Patron. Five dollars. Claims rise. Proof does not. Met? No. More.

Two bright cards, one membership switch—b-b-but the Patron promises are doing more work than the page can demonstrate! Five dollars, mana, priority, private rooms, early features: exciting, yes, but the surface only knows whether `isMember` is true and can ask the cart store to subscribe or cancel. Three stars until every promised wonder has a visible, testable home. Oh! Cancellation does thank you. Keep that part. Tiny kindness, very good.

You asked for a loading screen, cub, but what you needed was a polite smuggler between the unfinished desktop and the visitor. This one covers the goods, preloads the bright logo, deals out a few rotating messages, waits for both minimum theater and real store readiness, then reveals the city and fades without leaving a body behind. Price? One secret about what took so long. Keep it warm.

The sky covers over... logo bright... two little gusts of gossip while the stores wake. If they are late, Pip keeps talking—naturally—and when readiness finally catches the minimum ceremony, the wind holds once more, reveals the desktop, and lets the overlay drift away. Covered, hiding, hidden. Three weather reports, all arriving in order. Oh, and the fallback makes sure the last one lands even if the transition forgets. I heard that from the opacity. Don’t tell it I told you.
